If Teams Admin Center shows no camera device or mic input in the service telemetry, escalate to Desktop/Endpoint engineering to check driver, privacy permissions, and vendor USB driver reinstallation. The indexed records show Teams Test Call + client logs were the diagnostic artifacts used in prior AV issues [Data: Sources (37, 19)]. - RECOMMENDED: For the mic stuck at zero with a USB headset, also capture a short Windows Sound control test result and check whether other apps capture microphone input - that helps determine driver/Windows privacy vs Teams‑only failure (this diagnostic pattern appears in past endpoint tickets) [Data: Sources (37)]. C. Artifacts to collect and attach to the ticket (these are the exact items prior incidents used - collect before escalating) (These items match the evidence used in the records; hand them to the identity/engineer owner.) 1) SharePoint items (used in prior cases) [collect immediately] - Folder full URL and a full‑window screenshot of the error (address bar + timestamp). - The folder's Permission -> Advanced screenshot showing SP_Compliance_Main and the listed groups. - Which users are affected (UPNs) and a single working user sample who can access (to compare). - Evidence these were decisive: Permission view and screenshots were used in SharePoint access tickets [Data: Sources (20, 30)]. 2) Identity / directory exports (used in prior cases) - Azure AD group exports for SP_Project_IT, SP_Project_IT_v1, COMPLIANCE_TEAM, Compliance_Core (objectId, displayName, onPremisesSyncEnabled, members); Azure AD Connect sync run history covering the time of the org change. - Evidence: Microsoft Graph / Azure AD Connect exports and full sync were used to diagnose and fix collisions [Data: Sources (13)]. 3) Purview/Label data (used in prior cases) - Purview/AIP label config for "Confidential - Internal" (which group is authorized to decrypt), and timestamp when the label was applied to the folder. - Evidence: label application timing and allowed group mattered in the label incident [Data: Sources (30)]. 4) External uploader / server logs (used in prior cases) - Server SFTP/FTP logs for the failure interval; firewall/edge allow/deny logs for uploader IP; WinSCP client log output from your reproduction attempt. - Evidence: WinSCP tests + firewall changes were used in the FileZilla case [Data: Sources (34)]. 5) Teams / endpoint traces (used in prior cases) - Teams Admin Center call/device diagnostics for affected users, Teams desktop client logs collected immediately after reproduction, and a short statement of steps already tried (reboots, USB port swaps, browser variants). - Evidence: Teams client diagnostics and Admin Center traces were used in Teams device investigations [Data: Sources (37, 19)]. D. Where the indexed knowledge is insufficient / what you must provide - The indexed records do NOT contain your tenant's raw Azure AD sign‑in log rows, Azure AD Connect run timestamps for your overnight window, nor your server SFTP logs. I cannot confirm the concrete objectId GUIDs, the exact group membership diffs, nor the uploader's server‑side error lines from here - you must export those artifacts and attach them to the ticket for engineers to reproduce the identical evidence chain [Data: Sources (13, 30, 34)]. - Please provide (minimum) the SharePoint folder URL + permission screenshot, the list of affected UPNs, the Azure AD group export (objectId + members) for SP_Project_IT and COMPLIANCE_TEAM, Purview label config for "Confidential - Internal", the server SFTP logs for the uploader's failure timestamps, and the Teams client/Admin traces for the two Teams device problems. Prior investigations explicitly used those artifacts to conclude root cause and fix [Data: Sources (13, 30, 34, 37)]. E. One‑page recommended immediate action checklist to paste into your ticket (marked RECOMMENDED where not already documented) - RECOMMENDED: Escalate to BU Infrastructure - Identity and Core Engineering now (ticket owners in prior cases handed off to Identity/System Engineers for identical multi‑domain failures) [Data: Sources (13, 20, 30)]. - Documented action: Add TEST USER to SP_Project_IT and confirm whether that restores access; cite TI‑1569482 as precedent [Data: Sources (20)]. - RECOMMENDED: Export Azure AD group listings and Azure AD Connect sync history for the change window and attach to ticket (System Engineer 14 used these in the COMPLIANCE_TEAM case) [Data: Sources (13)]. - Documented action: Remove/reapply the label only after aligning Compliance_Core with ACL, then force token refresh for one affected user (documented label remediation pattern) [Data: Sources (30)]. - RECOMMENDED: Provide server SFTP logs + WinSCP client logs + firewall allow/deny logs for the uploader's IP/time window (FileZilla case used WinSCP & firewall analysis) [Data: Sources (34)]. - RECOMMENDED: Attach Teams Admin Center diagnostics and client logs for the camera/mic cases; run Teams Test Call and attach results (Teams test flows used in prior AV tickets) [Data: Sources (37, 19)]. F. Closing - what I will not invent and what I need from you - I cannot assert a single root cause from the indexed records alone because the symptoms span identity / label / sync, server/file‑transfer, and endpoint AV domains and the records show remediation required evidence from multiple artifacts (Azure AD Connect runs, Purview label config, server logs, Teams traces) that are not present here. To reach a definitive single root cause and apply the exact fixes used in prior incidents, please attach the artifacts listed above. The indexed precedents show the exact investigative sequence and remediation once those artifacts were present [Data: Sources (13, 30, 34, 37)]. If you want, I will now: - (A) produce the exact Azure AD / Graph query strings and the exact fields to export for the groups (objectId, onPremisesSyncEnabled, members) so your Identity admin can run them, and/or - (B) draft the minimal email text to send to the network/security team requesting the SFTP/server + firewall logs for the uploader's IP/time window. Tell me which you want me to generate and I will prepare it (these are recommended, actionable templates - not items extracted as already performed from the records).

question_text:
Hi IT, sorry but we have a very odd combination of issues since yesterday and multiple teams are blocked.

First: In D365 (Finance & Operations), Financial Reports is basically unusable. When we go to General Ledger -> Financial Reports -> Edit, it either errors or just spins forever. It is not only one person: several users report it. Also, older reports suddenly show the author as “unknown”, and when we try to open or create a report it sometimes says the report cannot be found. This happens in test and production, so we cannot even use test as a workaround.

Second: our new hotline numbers from the current marketing campaign are dead. Calls to the old main number still ring, but if a customer calls one of the new numbers it fails immediately. The carrier claims the calls reach our edge, but they never reach the users.

Third: the Basel office has a very specific behavior on external calls. The call connects, people can talk briefly, and then it drops after roughly 30 seconds almost every time. Internal calls are fine, and colleagues in other locations are fine. Someone mentioned that the phone network segment was changed recently.

Fourth: a few team leads say Teams no longer shows any pop-up banners on their laptops. Messages still arrive, but they only notice them once they open Teams. Their phones are fine. Windows updates ran last week and at least one person had Focus/Do Not Disturb enabled for a meeting.

Is this connected or just multiple incidents at once? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Below I use only what the indexed incident corpus records, and I clearly separate (A) what the records explicitly document about similar failures and prior remediations and (B) what I recommend next for this ticket when the records do not contain the needed live artifacts. For each factual statement I cite the most relevant indexed records. TL;DR - classification and immediate action - Based on the indexed records, this is not a routine single‑user fault. The mix of cross‑service symptoms (D365 Financial Reports spanning UAT+PROD, Teams SIP/Direct‑Routing/SBC behavior, firewall/NAT timing, and client notification issues) matches prior incidents that were escalated to engineering/identity/UC/Network teams. Recommended classification: second_level_support - escalate to Application (AOS/IIS/FRDB) owners, BU Infrastructure - Identity, Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing) owners, and Network/Firewall owners for coordinated investigation [Data: Sources (0); Sources (13); Sources (16)]. A. What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence you can rely on) 1) D365 / Financial Reports - Similar outages required AOS/IIS and Data‑Mart investigation, vendor escalation and (in prior cases) a Data‑Mart reset or AOS/IIS/TLS configuration changes. The Management‑Reporting incident recorded vendor escalation and changes to AOS/IIS/TLS + Data‑Mart actions as the remediation path [Data: Sources (0); Sources (56)]. 2) New hotline numbers (calls reach carrier -> edge but not users) - Prior cases where new number ranges failed at the carrier->edge showed the SBC translation/normalization table was missing entries; a temporary translation rule on the SBC validated the fix, then a permanent translation/routing update was applied [Data: Sources (8)]. 3) Basel ~30‑second call drops - Prior Basel incidents reproduced an SBC BYE at ~29-31s and traced root cause to NAT/session timer / UDP timeout mismatches or firewall/NAT behavior on the Voice VLAN; remedy was aligning NAT/session timeouts and SBC/session‑timer parameters (and firewall port rules where Media Bypass is used) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. 4) Teams desktop notifications / no pop‑up banners - Prior endpoint cases documenting missing Teams pop‑ups were resolved by checking Windows notification settings and Focus Assist, confirming Teams client notification permissions, and running Teams client diagnostics / Test Call. Windows updates and Focus/Do‑Not‑Disturb states were frequent contributors to missing desktop pop‑ups [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19); Sources (37)]. 5) Escalation pattern for multi‑system incidents - The indexed runbook/incident summaries show the canonical escalation path for multi‑service outages: CRM/portal intake -> BU Customer Services Run / Helpdesk triage -> escalate to Core Engineering / BU Infrastructure (Identity / UC / Network) when cross‑system or host/VM/dir‑sync impacts are present. Prior similar multi‑domain incidents were treated as second‑level and required coordinated cross‑team fixes [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34); Sources (13)]. B. Concrete, step‑by‑step resolution plan (mix of record‑backed steps and recommended actions) Below: for each step I indicate whether it is explicitly documented in the indexed records (Evidence) or whether it is a recommendation (Recommended) because the records don't include the live artifacts. Phase 0 - immediate triage (all four symptoms) - Action 0.1 - Gather and publish scope and timestamps now (Evidence + Recommended) - Collect: list of affected users (samples per symptom), first‑observed timestamp for each symptom (local & UTC if available), and whether the failures reproduce from multiple locations/browsers. Prior records show collecting this scope is standard triage and speeds escalation [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34)]. (Recommended - the records show teams always gathered scope.) - Action 0.2 - Open a cross‑functional bridge and assign owners (Evidence + Recommended) - Immediately open an escalated incident bridging Application (AOS/FRDB), Identity, Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing), and Network/Firewall teams and assign a single incident lead (Core Engineering / System Engineer). The indexed runbook explicitly recommends cross‑team bridgecalls for cross‑system outages [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34)]. (Recommended) Phase 1 - D365 Financial Reports (edit/view errors, authors = "unknown", "report not found") - What the records show worked previously (Evidence) - Prior Management‑Reporting / Financial‑Reports outages were resolved by investigating AOS/IIS/TLS configuration, Data‑Mart/FRDB service health and - when required - performing a Data‑Mart reset or AOS/IIS remediation and escalating to vendor/Microsoft if needed [Data: Sources (0); Sources (55)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended; map to prior-case actions) 1. Collect: - Error screenshots and full browser URL for a failing Edit action (several users). - Server‑side logs: AOS app logs, IIS logs for the Financial Reports site, and FRDB/Data‑Mart logs covering the primary incident window. (Recommended - prior tickets used exactly these artifacts) [Data: Sources (0)]. 2. Confirm whether any recent OS/patch/CLR/IISCRYPTO changes or TLS/cipher changes were applied to AOS/IIS in the 24-48 hours prior (Evidence: past incident required TLS/IIS changes) [Data: Sources (0)]. 3. Check whether "author = unknown" corresponds to any directory/group/object changes (Evidence + Recommended): export Azure AD audit log entries for group/object changes and SharePoint/D365 author mappings in the same window - previous incidents tied objectID/group changes to authorship/visibility anomalies [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30)]. 4. If AOS/IIS or FRDB errors point to service instability, escalate immediately to Application/Core Engineering and consider vendor/Microsoft escalation per precedent. (Evidence - prior incidents used vendor escalation) [Data: Sources (0)]. Phase 2 - Hotline numbers fail (carrier sees delivery to edge; users never ring) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - Missing SBC translation entries for new ranges produced INVITE -> 404 at the SBC; temporary translation rules validated the fix and permanent translation/routing table updates were applied. SBC + carrier traces were decisive evidence [Data: Sources (8)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. Collect from the carrier and SBC (Recommended): - Sample failed number(s), exact UTC timestamp(s) (caller, callee), and carrier trace IDs. - SBC SIP trace for those timestamps (full INVITE/response sequences) and the SBC translation table snapshot (pre/post) and any temporary translation-rule text applied during prior tests. (Recommended - this parallels TI‑1612036 approach) [Data: Sources (8)]. 2. Have UC/SBC engineer check SBC translation/normalization entries for those number ranges and apply a temporary translation rule to reproduce and validate routing - prior incident applied a temporary rule and then a permanent update [Data: Sources (8)]. (Recommended / Evidence) 3. Correlate with Teams Admin Center/voice provisioning telemetry to confirm whether the tenant assigned any voice policy provisioning changes to the affected users (Evidence: provisioning-script misassignment caused other voice outages) [Data: Sources (9)]. (Recommended) Phase 3 - Basel ~30s call drops (one‑way/short disconnect at ~29-31s) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - SBC logs showed SBC-originated BYE at ~29-31s; diagnosis traced this to NAT/UDP session timeouts on Voice VLAN or session‑timer mismatch. Fix used NAT timeout alignment and SBC/session‑timer changes (Basel case) or firewall port adjustments (Zurich case) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. Collect: - Representative failed call Call‑ID(s) and full SBC SIP trace (INVITE -> 200 OK -> ACK -> BYE) with exact timestamps. (Recommended - prior incidents used these) [Data: Sources (16)]. - Firewall/NAT logs for the client side and Voice VLAN, showing NAT session creation/expiry and UDP session timeout values for the same timestamps. (Recommended) [Data: Sources (16)]. 2. Correlate the SBC BYE timestamp with any NAT session expiry or firewall deny at that same time. If NAT session timeout is ~30s, align to Direct Routing blueprint (increase to match SBC session timers; prior fixes changed NAT timers or SBC timers to remove 30s drops) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended / Evidence) 3. If Media Bypass is enabled at the site, verify outbound firewall dynamic RTP range allowed; Zurich examples show restricting dynamic RTP to only 3478-3481 caused audio issues until corrected [Data: Sources (23)]. (Recommended / Evidence) Phase 4 - Teams desktop pop‑ups missing (messages arrive only when Teams opened) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - Prior Teams client notification issues resolved by verifying Windows Focus Assist / Do Not Disturb, Teams notification settings, Teams Test Call, and Teams client updates. A recorded case found Focus Assist or app permission settings to be the cause and resolved locally [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19); Sources (37)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. On affected laptops: confirm Windows Focus Assist / Do Not Disturb is off and that Teams has Notifications permission; capture a Teams client log and run a Teams Test Call / Notification test and record timestamp. (Recommended - prior tickets used these checks) [Data: Sources (37); Sources (19); Sources (42)]. 2. If many users were updated last week, check whether a Windows Update or Teams client upgrade coincided with the symptom; if so, collect client update timestamp(s) and consider rolling back or reapplying the Teams client fix per vendor guidance. (Recommended) C. Correlation: single root cause vs multiple co‑incidents - what the records say - The indexed corpus shows that when multiple independent subsystems (identity/group mappings, provisioning scripts, Direct Routing/SBC, print servers) fail after an org‑chart/group change, the pattern commonly indicates a provisioning/directory sync ripple that affects many downstream services and justifies second‑level escalation and cross‑team remediation [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9); Sources (16)]. (Evidence) - Therefore: because you have cross‑service impact (D365 + telephony + site‑specific UC + Teams clients), the pattern aligns with prior cross‑system incidents that were escalated to second_level_support and required coordinated engineering work [Data: Sources (13); Sources (3)]. (Evidence -> classification recommendation) D. Concrete owners and who to notify now (recommended; aligned to past practice) - Immediately notify and assemble: - Application / D365 owner + Core Engineering (AOS/IIS/FRDB) - per Management Reports precedent [Data: Sources (0)]. (Recommended) - BU Infrastructure - Identity (Azure AD / Azure AD Connect / provisioning) - to review group/object collisions and provisioning script effects [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9)]. (Recommended) - Unified Communications / SBC owner - request SBC SIP traces and translation table review [Data: Sources (8)]. (Recommended) - Network / Firewall team - request NAT/session timeout values and firewall change history; check Voice VLAN NAT policies and RTP port rules [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended) - Endpoint/Desktop support - run Teams client/Focus Assist checks and capture client logs for Teams pop‑up issue [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. (Recommended) E. Minimum evidence to attach to the incident ticket (so 2nd‑level teams can act immediately) - D365: AOS IIS logs (primary window), FRDB/DataMart error logs, screenshots of the error, and the list of affected users and exact timestamps [Data: Sources (0)]. (Recommended) - Telephony (hotline): sample called number(s), UTC timestamps, carrier trace IDs, and SBC SIP trace(s) for the same times, plus SBC translation table export and Teams Admin Center policy assignment snapshots [Data: Sources (8); Sources (9)]. (Recommended) - Basel UC: SBC SIP traces showing BYE at ~29-31s, firewall/NAT logs for the Voice VLAN showing UDP/NAT session timeouts, and the site change ticket that moved the network segment (if any) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended) - Teams endpoint: Teams client logs and Windows Event logs for Focus Assist/Notifications around the sample test time; list of affected hostnames and exact update timestamps [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. (Recommended) F. Final classification (explicit) - Classify this ticket as second_level_support (escalate to engineering/identity/UC/network). Rationale: prior indexed incidents with the same multi‑domain scope were escalated and required hands‑on engineering (Azure AD Connect / provisioning fixes; SBC/firewall parameter changes; AOS/IIS/Data Mart actions) rather than first‑line fixes [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9); Sources (0); Sources (16)]. (Evidence) G. If the indexed records do NOT contain enough information to fully resolve the ticket - statement - The indexed records document the workflows, past root causes and the artifacts investigators used, but they do NOT contain your tenant's live logs (AOS/IIS/FRDB error logs, SBC SIP traces, firewall/NAT logs, Azure AD sign‑in logs, or actual provisioning CI job runs). To resolve this incident now we must collect and attach the live artifacts listed above. The prior incidents show those exact artifacts were decisive [Data: Sources (0); Sources (8); Sources (16); Sources (13)]. (Explicit statement) H. Suggested immediate message to paste into your ticket (copy‑paste - recommended) - "Escalate to second_level_support: Application (AOS/IIS/FRDB), Identity (Azure AD / Azure AD Connect / Provisioning), Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing), and Network/Firewall required. Critical artifacts requested: (1) AOS/IIS+FRDB logs for 48h window around failure; (2) sample failed hotline numbers + carrier traceIDs + SBC SIP traces and SBC translation table; (3) SBC SIP traces + firewall NAT/session logs for Basel sample calls showing BYE ~29-31s; (4) Teams client logs + Windows Event logs and Focus Assist state for affected hosts. Please attach these artifacts and confirm ownership for each scope so we can start cross‑correlation. Classification: second_level_support. [References: similar prior resolutions: Financial Reports/AOS remediation (TI‑722511) [Data: Sources (0)]; SBC translation-rule remediation (TI‑1612036) [Data: Sources (8)]; Basel NAT/session timeout BYE ~29-31s (TI‑1604879) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]; SharePoint/group/Entra remediation (TI‑1583327) [Data: Sources (13)].]" (Recommended) - End - If you want I will: - Draft the exact log export filters and filenames to hand to each owner (AOS/IIS, SBC, Firewall, Azure AD Connect, Teams client). (Recommended - I will produce them on request.) - Or, if you can attach one concrete artifact now (one SBC SIP trace or the AOS IIS error log for the Financial Reports error), I will analyze and map it to the most likely remediation path shown in the indexed incidents.

question_text:
Hi IT, I need help because we have multiple problems at once and people are losing it.

In Zurich (training room) external calls in Microsoft Teams still connect and show as "Connected", the other side can hear us, but on our side it is totally silent (no audio at all). We already tried different headsets and speakers and it makes no difference. Internal Teams calls are fine.

At the same time in Basel the office is basically unable to print. One new colleague literally sees zero printers on their laptop even though everyone else in the same room can print normally. Another colleague can see the printer, but the moment they hit Print it instantly says access denied and nothing even shows up anywhere. And for the rest of us: jobs appear in the print queue but then just sit there forever like it is stuck.

We did the usual stuff (restart laptop, unplug dock, change USB port, try a different app) but nothing changed. Does this look connected or just bad timing, and please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Below I use only the indexed incident records to (A) state what the archive explicitly documents about these symptom classes, (B) give a concise step‑by‑step remediation plan that mirrors what was actually done in prior incidents (with citations), and (C) mark recommended actions that go beyond the archived actions. I do not assume access to your tenant or make any unrecorded claims. If you want me to assemble the exact text to paste into your ticketing portal (one paragraph per escalation target + list of attachments), say so and I'll prepare it. --- 1) What the indexed records explicitly document about these symptom classes - Teams one‑way / no‑audio for external calls was repeatedly caused by network/SBC/media‑path issues (firewall blocking RTP or NAT/session timeouts) rather than endpoint hardware in prior cases; investigators used Teams Admin Center + SBC SIP traces + firewall/NAT logs and validated fixes by temporary port openings and SBC parameter changes [This is documented] [Data: Sources (23); Sources (16); Sources (8)]. - Simultaneous printing problems in other incidents were caused by several distinct directory/GPO and print‑path faults: AD/group nesting causing wrong location GPO/printer mapping; an explicit Deny ACE on a queue blocking a user; printers moved to a new PRINTER VLAN without the relay/routing allow‑list; and, for SAP flows, Windows print‑server SMB/share publishing broken after VM restore - remediation actions in prior cases included removing Deny ACEs, forcing GPO (gpupdate /force) and re‑enabling the print‑server network share (SAP case) [This is documented] [Data: Sources (2); Sources (3); Sources (22); Sources (28); Sources (39)]. - When multiple subsystems failed after an org/group change, the indexed records consistently routed work to second‑level teams (Identity / BU Infrastructure - M365, Unified Communications / SBC engineers, and Core Engineering for print servers) rather than being resolved at first‑line only [This is documented and forms the historical escalation pattern] [Data: Sources (13); Sources (8); Sources (28)]. --- 2) Immediate, evidence‑backed triage (do these steps first). Each step cites the prior case(s) that used the same action. A. Teams (Zurich training room) - triage steps (documented in prior incidents) 1. Gather a representative failing call sample: timestamp (UTC), caller UPN/extension, called number, and the local client clock time when the UI shows "Connected -> silent" [used by prior responders to align traces] [Data: Sources (23); Sources (8)]. 2. Pull Teams Admin Center call trace for that timestamp and the per‑call correlationId(s) [used as the control‑plane correlate in prior cases] [Data: Sources (8)]. 3. Request SBC SIP trace for the same call (full INVITE/200OK/ACK/SDP and final BYE) and include Call‑ID and timestamps - prior incidents used these traces to find BYE at ~29-31s or 404 routing responses [Data: Sources (8); Sources (16)]. 4. Request firewall/NAT logs for the same time window showing UDP/RTP flows and NAT session events (session creation and expiry) and any firewall change entries in the recent change window; prior Zurich/Basel incidents used firewall/NAT logs to prove blocked RTP or 30s NAT expiry [Data: Sources (23); Sources (16)]. 5. Correlate SBC BYE time with any NAT expiry or firewall deny entry; if BYE coincides with a 30s NAT timeout or a deny of RTP ports, that is the documented cause and the remedy is to align NAT/session timers or open the dynamic RTP range per the Direct Routing blueprint [Documented pattern and remedy] [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (These are the same investigative artifacts and validation steps shown effective in TI‑1602241 / TI‑1604879 / TI‑1612036.) [Data: Sources (23,16,8)] B. Printing (Basel) - triage steps (documented in prior incidents) 1. Capture example user evidence (for each symptom type): a) user who sees zero printers - screenshot of "Printers & scanners" and full Windows address bar + UPN; b) user who gets "Access denied" on print - screenshot of error and the targeted queue name; c) job that is stuck - server‑side queue snapshot (queued jobs with owner, document name, JobID) [prior cases recorded these artifacts as essential] [Data: Sources (39); Sources (3); Sources (27)]. 2. Export the affected users' AD group membership (the membership set used by GPOs) and the group(s) that map to printers (location groups such as PRT‑Basel, ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP). Prior Finance/Printer incidents showed incorrect nesting (Finance -> Zurich) caused wrong default printer [Data: Sources (2); Sources (39)]. 3. On an affected workstation, force Group Policy refresh and re‑login (gpupdate /force + sign out/in) and record whether the printer mapping appears - this was the exact validation step in multiple resolved cases [Data: Sources (39)]. 4. On the print server(s): inspect queue ACLs for explicit Deny ACEs and remove after confirming intent (Basel explicit Deny case). If spooler shows paused queues, activate queue and restart jobs (documented remediation) [Data: Sources (3); Sources (27)]. 5. If jobs disappear from the Windows queue (or SAP printing is involved), check whether printers were moved into a PRINTER VLAN and whether the print server can reach the device (ping) and whether the VLAN IP is present in the SMTP relay allow‑list for Scan‑to‑Mail; prior VLAN moves required routing/firewall adjustments and SMTP allow‑list edits [Data: Sources (22); Sources (6)]. 6. If SAP print paths are used, identify the SAP print target -> Windows queue mapping and, if the printserver was recently restored/updated, validate the SMB network‑share publishing state. In a documented SAP case, disabling and re‑enabling the network share on VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 released pending SAP jobs [Data: Sources (28); Sources (7)]. (These steps mirror what Core Engineering / Print and Network teams did in the recorded print incidents.) [Data: Sources (28,22,3,39)] --- 3) Artifacts to gather NOW (explicitly supported by the records) Collect these and attach to the ticket before escalation - prior incident notes show these accelerate diagnosis: - Teams/UC: sample call timestamp (UTC), Teams Admin Center call trace/correlationId, SBC SIP trace for that call (INVITE/200/SDP/BYE), firewall/NAT logs for RTP ports and NAT session-timeouts for the same window [Data: Sources (8, 16, 23)]. - Print: screenshots from affected users (Printers pane, Access denied), AD group membership export for affected users, print server queue snapshot (JobID, Owner, document name), print server event logs (PrintService operational EVTX) and queue ACL screenshot, and network/VLAN/routing allow‑list for printers - if SAP is involved, SAP spool IDs for the affected jobs and which Windows queue they mapped to [Data: Sources (39, 3, 27, 28, 22, 7)]. - Identity/context: Azure AD sign‑in entries for a sample affected user (token claim list), and Azure AD Connect sync run history covering the suspected change window if you suspect group/object changes (prior investigations used these to prove overwrite) [Data: Sources (31, 13)]. Cite these artifacts in the ticket and attach them: prior cases used them to get to root cause rapidly [Data: Sources (8); Sources (28); Sources (13)]. --- 4) Immediate classification recommendation (supported by records) - Classify this incident: second_level_support. Rationale: simultaneous, cross‑domain failures (Unified Communications media + directory/GPO + print server) match prior incidents that required second‑line/system‑engineer escalation (Identity team for group/sync issues; UC/SBC and Network for media issues; Core Engineering for print‑server fixes) rather than being resolvable entirely by first‑line steps [Documented precedent: TI‑1583327, TI‑1602241, TI‑931293] [Data: Sources (13); Sources (23); Sources (28)]. --- 5) Documented remediation examples you should reference (paste into ticket) (These are actions actually performed in archived incidents; list them so the receiving engineers see precedent.) - If SBC logs show INVITE->200 OK but no client RTP and firewall logs show blocked dynamic RTP ports -> open dynamic RTP range per Direct Routing blueprint and validate with a Teams Test Call (temporary open to validate, then apply permanent rule) [Documented fix from Zurich incidents] [Data: Sources (23); Sources (5)]. - If SBC shows BYE at ~29-31s correlated with NAT/UDP expiry -> align NAT/session timeout with Direct Routing blueprint (increase from 30s to recommended value) and retest (shown in Basel case) [Documented] [Data: Sources (16)]. - If a print queue has an explicit Deny ACE -> remove the Deny ACE and test print (documented Basel case) [Documented] [Data: Sources (3)]. - If new user sees zero printers but others in same room print -> validate AD group membership and force GPO (gpupdate /force + re‑login) as documented remediation in several tickets [Documented] [Data: Sources (39)]. - If SAP jobs are stuck after a VM update/restore -> disable and re‑enable the printer network share on the SAP printserver (VRINFRGERPRNT03/04) to release queued SAP jobs - this was the recorded corrective action in a PRIO HIGH case [Documented] [Data: Sources (28); Sources (7)]. Do not present these as generic commands - they are documented prior actions; attach the referenced ticket ids when you ask engineers to reuse the same fix path [Data: Sources (28); Sources (3); Sources (23); Sources (39)]. --- 6) Recommended coordination / escalation (clearly marked as recommended) (These items are recommended operational steps - they are not claimed as done in the indexed records.) - Immediately escalate and open cross‑functional bridge/call with: UC/SBC owner (include SBC log owner), Network/Firewall owner, Identity / BU Infrastructure - M365, and Core Engineering / Print owner. Attach the artifacts listed in §3. (recommended) - For identity correlation, ask Identity to run Microsoft Graph membership exports and Azure AD sign‑in query for the time window you supply; if you cannot share logs here, ask them to paste the key lines showing token claims (COMPLIANCE_TEAM vs Compliance_Core) for the sample users. (recommended) - If you need an authoritative change rollback (e.g., remove a recent org/group change), request Identity/AD to produce Azure AD Connect run history and consider a conservative rollback plan only after cross-team validation. (recommended) These recommendations reflect the operational escalation pattern and evidence collected in the historical incidents [Data: Sources (13); Sources (8); Sources (28)]. --- 7) Final checklist you can paste into your ticket now (copy/paste) (Use this to speed the handoff; items in [] should be filled in.) - Symptom summary (one line): Zurich - external Teams calls show "Connected" but no inbound audio (other side hears us). Basel - new user sees zero printers; another user gets "Access denied"; others' jobs stuck. [Provided by reporter]. - Classification: second_level_support - escalate to UC/SBC, Network, Identity, Core Engineering. [Supported: Sources (13,23,28)] - Required artifacts (attach): 1) Teams: sample failing call time (UTC) + Teams Admin Center call trace/correlationId + SBC SIP trace INVITE->200->SDP->BYE for same call + firewall/NAT logs for RTP/NAT session timeframe [Data: Sources (8,16,23)]. 2) Printing: screenshots (Printers pane / Access denied), AD group membership export for affected users, print server queue snapshot (JobID/Owner/Document/Time), print server event logs (PrintService EVTX), and (if SAP) SAP spool IDs + target mapping [Data: Sources (39,3,27,28,7)]. 3) Identity: sample UPN(s) and time window for sign‑in log export (Azure AD sign‑in logs + Azure AD Connect sync runs) [Data: Sources (31,13)]. - Short next steps (who to call): open bridge with UC/SBC owner (SBC logs), Network team (firewall/NAT), Identity (Graph + sign‑in logs), Core Engineering/Print (spooler/SMB/share). [Pattern documented: Sources (23, 13, 28)] - Attach precedent tickets: TI‑1602241 (Zurich media), TI‑1604879 (Basel 30s), TI‑1612036 (SBC translation/new numbers), TI‑931293 (SAP printing/VM restore) for your on‑call engineers to reference. [Data: Sources (23,16,8,28)] --- 8) If the indexed records do NOT contain enough to complete the fix - They do not contain your tenant's live SBC trace files, firewall logs, print‑server EVTX or your Azure AD Connect run logs. You must collect and attach those artifacts (exact files and timestamps) to allow the same evidence‑driven steps shown in the records to be executed here. I cannot run those exports for you. This limitation is explicit in the indexed corpus (prior tickets referenced those artifacts but did not embed the raw traces in the indexed summaries) [Data: Sources (8,16,28,13)]. --- If you want I will now: - (A) draft the short bridge invitation text (one paragraph) that lists the attachments above and names the specialist teams to invite; or - (B) draft the exact, copy/paste log‑request messages for the SBC owner and for the Identity team (Graph + Azure AD sign‑in exports) including the specific incident windows I cited. Tell me which and I'll produce it (that will be a recommended, actionable output based on the indexed precedents).

question_text:
Hi IT, we need help asap because it feels like the whole company is falling apart since the weekend changes. I’m trying to describe it without guessing, but it’s a mess.

1) In D365 (F&O) the Financial Reports / Report Designer thing is basically broken. It either loads forever or errors out. What’s super strange: older reports suddenly show the author as "unknown" and sometimes it says a report "cannot be found" even when we used it last week. This happens in both test and prod, so we can’t even use test as a workaround.

2) Sales: the desktop mail app is acting up. People can receive emails, but anything they send just sits in Outbox and later fails with a connection-style error. If they send the same email from the browser mailbox it goes through. Mobile seems fine too.

3) External partners: in Teams they are shown as Offline all day even while they are actively writing to us. Chats arrive late and sometimes calls to them do not ring. Internals are fine.

4) Zurich site: external calls in Teams connect, the other side says they hear us, but on our side it’s dead silent (zero audio). We swapped headsets and speakers and it’s the same. Internal calls are ok.

5) Basel site: external calls connect and then drop after about half a minute almost every time, like a timer. Internal calls are ok.

6) Also with the new marketing hotline numbers: calls to the old main number work, but calls to the new number range fail instantly. The provider claims they deliver the call to our edge.

7) Printing is chaos too, but it’s not one single thing. A new employee in Basel sees literally no printers at all, even though everyone next to them can print. Another user can see the printer but gets "Access denied" immediately when printing. And for the rest of us: jobs show up in the queue and then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

8) One more: in Basel the main office printer sometimes shows jobs for a second and then they vanish, while other printers in the building still work.

People keep blaming laptops, docks, and Windows updates, but all of this started at basically the same time after the weekend work. Is this one connected change or just unlucky timing? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Below I separate (A) what the indexed incident records explicitly document (symptoms, root causes, diagnostic artifacts and the exact remedial steps that were actually performed in prior incidents) from (B) recommended next steps where the records do not provide the live artifacts needed to fully resolve your case. I end with a short, prioritized remediation/escallation checklist you can action now. A - What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence you can cite) - Overall classification precedent: multi‑service, multi‑site incidents that cross identity/mail/Direct‑Routing/print domains were handled as engineering (2nd‑level) incidents and required cross‑team escalation in the indexed cases [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 23, 28)]. 1) D365 Financial Reports / Report Designer - Prior incidents with the same symptom class required application‑team and vendor escalation, FRDB/Data‑Mart resets and Microsoft support case opening (example: FRDB/Data Mart remediation and Microsoft support case #2404050050000982). The recorded remediation included FRDB / Data Mart actions and escalation to Microsoft support [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. 2) Desktop Outlook clients: messages stuck in Outbox but OWA/Mobile OK - Prior cases showed Exchange Event Log evidence with "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" and tenant Security Defaults / Conditional Access blocking SMTP AUTH for legacy clients; remediation recorded in the ticket was to selectively re‑enable SMTP AUTH or adjust Conditional Access and then monitor mailflow [Data: Sources (32)]. 3) Teams external presence / chats shown Offline - A documented incident (TI‑1607314) found tenant External Access / Presence settings (AllowPublicUsers, AllowFederatedUsers, EnablePresencePrivacyMode / Presence Sharing policy) had been tightened and that reverting/adjusting these settings (via Teams admin / PowerShell) restored external presence; Teams Admin Center / Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration were used during troubleshooting [Data: Sources (26)]. 4) Zurich - one‑way audio on external Teams calls - The Zurich one‑way audio case was traced to a weekend firewall security‑hardening change that limited outbound UDP ports (allowed only 3478-3481 and blocked the dynamic RTP range). Temporary opening of the wider RTP range restored media in tests; a permanent correction aligned the firewall to the Direct‑Routing / media‑flow blueprint [Data: Sources (23); Reports (5)]. 5) Basel - external calls drop after ~30 seconds - The Basel case was diagnosed as a NAT/UDP session timeout mismatch on the Voice VLAN (session timeout ~30s), validated with SBC/session tests, and fixed by aligning NAT/session timeouts to the Direct‑Routing blueprint; SBC logs showed SBC‑originated BYE at ~29-31s during the failing calls [Data: Sources (16); Reports (10)]. 6) New hotline / new number‑range failing instantly - The recorded investigation shows the carrier confirmed delivery of calls to the SIP trunk/SBC, but the SBC translation/normalization table lacked the new range. Applying a temporary translation rule validated call delivery; a permanent translation/routing update was then applied and validated [Data: Sources (8)]. The indexed records do not contain the carrier CDR files themselves - only the carrier confirmation and SBC log findings [Data: Sources (8)]. 7) Printing: missing printers, Access Denied, jobs vanish / stuck - Multiple documented patterns and fixes appear in the records: - GPO not applied / new user: gpupdate /force + re‑login successfully applied printer mappings in user sessions [Data: Sources (39)]. - Queue paused / jobs stuck: re‑activate server queue and restart jobs (worked in a recorded ticket) [Data: Sources (27)]. - Printer queue/share corruption after Windows update + VM restore (SAP print servers VRINFRGERPRNT03/04): spooler restart did not help, a VM restore was performed and then administrators cleared SAP backlog by disabling/re‑enabling the printer network share - that action cleared queued jobs and restored printing in that incident [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. - Access Denied was resolved in one case by removing an explicit Deny ACE on the queue [Data: Sources (3)]. - Basel printer jobs briefly appearing then vanishing were tied to reachability/routing/firewall between print server and the printer; a firewall/routing adjustment restored printing [Data: Sources (22)]. B - What the indexed records do NOT contain (limits / where live evidence is required) - The indexed records do not contain your tenant's live D365/AOS/IIS logs, Exchange Event Log exports for your hosts, SBC logs/pcaps for the weekend window, firewall/WAF change diffs from your devices, or the print‑server Event Log/spooler crash dumps for your environment. Those live artifacts are required to prove root cause and to take a targeted fix. The prior cases document the investigative artefacts that were used, but they do not contain your environment's current logs [Data: Sources (0, 8, 23, 28, 32)]. C - Step‑by‑step resolution plan (mixing documented prior‑case fixes where available and recommended actions where the records do not contain the live data) High‑level classification (documented precedent) - Based on the breadth of services affected (D365, Exchange/SMTP, Teams/Direct‑Routing, SBC/carrier handoff, printing + AD/GPO), prior similar cases were handled as second_level_support and required coordinated engineering teams: Identity/AD, Exchange/Mail, Unified Communications / SBC, Network/Firewall, Print Infrastructure and Application/D365 teams [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 23, 28)]. Recommendation (classification): classify this ticket as second_level_support (escalate immediately). This matches the precedent in the indexed incidents [Data: Sources (0); Sources (8); Sources (16)]. Step 1 - Open cross‑functional incident bridge & preserve evidence (recommended but matches prior practice) - Action (recommended): Immediately open a 2nd‑level incident bridge and notify Network/Firewall, UC/SBC, Mail/Exchange Hybrid, D365/AppOps, Print Infrastructure/Core Engineering, and Identity/AzureAD owners. The indexed cases show these domains were engaged in prior multi‑service incidents [Data: Sources (0, 8, 23, 28)]. - Evidence preservation (required): request and preserve the following for the weekend window (these are the exact artefacts used in prior incidents): - D365/App: AOS server logs, Report Designer error traces, Data Mart / FRDB export or reset logs, and the Microsoft support package if already opened [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. - Exchange/Mail: on‑prem Exchange Event Log extracts (filter for "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" and related SMTP auth events), Message Trace exports, hybrid connector logs, and Conditional Access audit events [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams/UC: Teams Admin Center diagnostics, Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration output, Teams call records/call analytics (if available), SBC SIP traces (INVITE/200/404/BYE), and SBC CDRs - note that prior incidents used SBC logs and carrier confirmations as primary evidence [Data: Sources (26); Sources (8); Sources (16)]. - Network/Firewall/WAF: firewall/WAF change logs and configuration diffs for the weekend (device audit export), and firewall logs showing blocked RTP/UDP for Zurich case reference [Data: Sources (15); Sources (23)]. - Printing: PrintServer System/Application/PrintService event logs, spooler state, any kernel crash dumps (if BSODs), published SMB share enable/disable timestamps, and GPO application traces (gpupdate attempts). Previous print incidents explicitly asked for these [Data: Sources (28, 29, 39)]. Step 2 - Reproduce / validate quick fixes (documented, perform in controlled tests) - D365: attempt to reproduce the Report Designer failure in a controlled session and capture AOS/IIS error logs. Prior cases escalated when report metadata (authors = "unknown") and FRDB corruption were present; FRDB/Data Mart reset and Microsoft escalation were the recorded remediation path where in‑place recovery failed [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. - If a vendor case does not already exist, open Microsoft support (the records cite a Microsoft case for a similar problem) [Data: Sources (0)]. - Exchange/Outlook send failures: confirm that the same message sends successfully from OWA/mobile (as you observed). Collect the on‑prem Exchange Event Log entries with the "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" string if present - prior ticket used that exact Event Log string as the diagnostic trigger and then adjusted Conditional Access/SMTP AUTH settings [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams presence: run Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration and review External Access/Presence settings. In the recorded case, adjusting AllowPublicUsers / EnablePresencePrivacyMode / Presence Sharing fixed external presence for partners [Data: Sources (26)]. - Zurich audio: run a controlled test call while temporarily opening the broader dynamic RTP port range (as a test) - prior Zurich case used temporary port relaxation to prove the firewall was blocking media; once validated, apply permanent firewall config aligned to the Direct‑Routing blueprint [Data: Sources (23); Reports (5)]. - Basel ~30s drops: capture SBC logs for failing calls (SIP traces). Prior incidents showed SBC BYE at ~29-31s and were remediated by aligning NAT/session timeouts and Session‑Expires to the Direct‑Routing blueprint [Data: Sources (16); Reports (10)]. - New hotline numbers: check SBC translation/normalization table for the new range; the indexed record shows a missing translation entry caused immediate failures and adding a temporary translation rule validated delivery, then a permanent update was applied [Data: Sources (8)]. If carrier correlation is needed, request carrier CDRs (records show carrier confirmed delivery but the carrier traces were not attached in the indexed tickets) [Data: Sources (8)]. Step 3 - Apply documented remediations where prior cases recorded success (do these only after testing) - Teams/Firewall (Zurich): if temporary RTP port opening restores media in tests, then align firewall rules to allow the dynamic RTP range used by Teams Media Bypass per the Direct‑Routing blueprint and monitor 48 h (the Zurich record did exactly this and validated stability after 48h) [Data: Sources (23); Reports (5)]. - SBC / new numbers: if SBC translation entry is missing, apply the temporary translation rule to validate calls, then make the permanent translation/routing table change and validate with test calls as recorded in TI‑1612036 [Data: Sources (8)]. - Basel ~30s drops: update NAT/session timeout and SBC session parameters to match blueprint (documented fix) and validate with test calls (prior case applied permanent timeout change after temporary test proved success) [Data: Sources (16)]. - Printing: when symptoms map to documented cases: - If queue is "Paused": reactivate queue and restart jobs (documented success) [Data: Sources (27)]. - If GPO mapping missing for a user: perform gpupdate /force and require a re‑login (documented successful step) [Data: Sources (39)]. - If SMB/share corruption suspected after a VM restore/Windows update (SAP case): disabling and re‑enabling the network share cleared the backlog in the prior incident; if BSODs followed a restore, preserve crash dumps and escalate to Core Engineering for forensic analysis / Windows rollback discussion [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. - If "Access Denied" for a single user: check for explicit Deny ACE and remove if found (documented fix) [Data: Sources (3)]. - If jobs appear then vanish and ping fails from server to printer: check network/firewall routing between print server and printer VLAN - firewall rule change restored reachability in the Basel case [Data: Sources (22)]. Step 4 - If fixes above do not restore service, escalate with preserved artifacts (documented escalation pattern) - D365: if in‑place remediations fail, escalate to Microsoft (the prior incident opened a Microsoft support case and performed FRDB/Data Mart actions) [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. - Exchange: if Event Log 535 entries persist after Conditional Access adjustments, escalate to Mail/Infra engineering with exported Event Log lines, Message Trace and hybrid connector logs (the prior case used those artifacts to conclude tenant Security Defaults/CA changes were the cause) [Data: Sources (32)]. - UC/SBC: if media or call‑routing persists, escalate UC/Network with SBC SIP traces, SBC CDRs, Teams Admin Center call records and carrier CDRs - the prior SBC/number incidents coordinated carrier + SBC + UC engineering [Data: Sources (8); Sources (16); Sources (23)]. - Print: if VM restore caused BSODs or persistent corruption, escalate to Core Engineering / virtualization team with VM snapshot/restore context (INTER12203 / INTER12204 were recorded restore contexts in the SAP case), crash dumps and hypervisor logs (the SAP case explicitly requested rollback/forensic capture) [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. D - Immediate prioritized checklist you can execute now (practical, evidence‑based) (Each line shows which prior incident used the same step.) 1) Open 2nd‑level incident and invite: Network/Firewall (perimeter), Unified Communications / SBC, Mail (Exchange), D365/AppOps, Print Infrastructure / Core Engineering, Identity/Azure AD. Prior multi‑domain incidents used this cross‑team escalation pattern [Data: Sources (0); Sources (8); Sources (23); Sources (28)]. 2) Collect and attach these artefacts to the incident ticket (exact items cited in prior investigations): - D365: AOS/IIS error traces and Report Designer error dumps; Data Mart / FRDB logs; any vendor/Microsoft SR numbers [Data: Sources (0)]. - Exchange: Windows Event Log export around failure times (look for 535 5.7.3), Message Trace, hybrid connector logs and Conditional Access audit logs [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams / UC: Teams Admin Center call diagnostics, Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration output, SBC SIP traces/CDR slices for failing call timestamps, and firewall/WAF diffs for the weekend window [Data: Sources (26); Sources (8); Sources (23)]. - Network/Firewall: firewall device config diff / audit export for the weekend (the Zurich WAF/firewall change was proven by device audit/inspection in the prior case) [Data: Sources (15); Sources (23)]. - Print: PrintService operational logs, spooler state, gpupdate logs, queue state, SMB share enable/disable timestamps, VM restore snapshot IDs and any crash dumps (SAP case used these artifacts) [Data: Sources (28, 29, 39)]. 3) Run the small, quick validation steps (documented fixes used successfully before): gpupdate /force and re‑login for affected users (GPO printer missing case) [Data: Sources (39)]; re‑activate paused queues and restart jobs (TI‑1621044) [Data: Sources (27)]; temporarily open RTP range for Zurich tests (documented successful test) [Data: Sources (23)]; apply temporary SBC translation rule to validate new hotline delivery (TI‑1612036) [Data: Sources (8)]. Do these only as controlled test steps and capture logs. 4) If you need carrier correlation for the hotline numbers, request carrier CDRs/SIP traces for the specific sample numbers and UTC windows - the indexed record shows carrier confirmation was used, but no carrier CDRs were attached in that case; asking the carrier for per‑call traces is the recommended next step when SBC logs need correlation [Data: Sources (8)]. E - Explicit statement of current limits and final recommendation (honest about what the indexed records cannot do) - The indexed knowledge set documents the kinds of root causes and the exact remediation steps that were effective in prior, similar incidents (examples and remediation recorded above). However, those records do NOT contain your site's live logs, firewall diffs, SBC traces/pcaps, Exchange Event Log exports or the print‑server crash dumps needed to prove the root cause for your environment. Therefore I cannot conclusively declare a single change as the universal root cause from the indexed corpus - the records show multiple distinct change events (firewall/WAF edits, NAT/session timeout adjustments, tenant policy changes, SBC translation omissions, Windows update + VM restore) that in prior tickets caused different subsets of your symptoms [Data: Sources (15, 23, 16, 8, 28)]. - Practical conclusion (evidence‑based): do NOT treat this as first‑line only. The correct classification is second_level_support and you should escalate immediately to the engineering owners named above. Prior incidents with the same symptom breadth were escalated and resolved by those teams [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 23, 28)]. If you want I will: - Draft the incident‑bridge invite and the exact artifact request text (one‑click copy/paste) for each owning team (Network, UC/SBC, Exchange, D365/AppOps, Print/Core Eng). This is a recommended next step (not verbatim in prior tickets) but it follows the exact evidence collection patterns used in the recorded incidents. - Or, if you prefer, confirm which of the listed logs you can gather now and I will annotate next actions for each log artifact (what to look for in the output, and which team to hand it to). Key references (examples from the incident corpus) - D365 / Report Designer & FRDB remediation and Microsoft escalation [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. - Exchange SMTP AUTH / Event Log 535 and Conditional Access remediation [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams presence / federation policy change and Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration checks [Data: Sources (26)]. - Zurich firewall RTP port blocking -> temporary open -> permanent fix per Direct‑Routing blueprint [Data: Sources (23)]. - Basel session timeout / SBC BYE at ~29-31s -> NAT/session timeout alignment [Data: Sources (16)]. - New hotline / SBC translation omission -> temporary translation rule -> permanent routing update; carrier confirmed delivery to SBC (but records do not contain carrier CDR attachments) [Data: Sources (8)]. - SAP printserver VM restore + spooler/network‑share corruption -> disabling/re‑enabling share cleared backlog; spooler restart alone did not fix; crash dumps and forensic capture were requested in that prior case [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. If you confirm you want the incident bridge opened now, give me (A) the primary incident contact(s) and (B) confirmation you authorize 2nd‑level escalation and I will produce the artifact request emails and the immediate triage sequence you should run.
